mnemonics: Cantor's Diagonal Argument: Imagine an infinite list of decimal expansions. Construct a new number by changing the nth digit in the nth number. This new number can't be on the list, proving the reals are uncountable.
keyPoints: Cantor's Theorem states that for any set, the power set has a strictly greater cardinality.
It implies there is no 'largest' infinity; there is an infinite hierarchy of infinities.
The proof uses a diagonalization argument, showing that no function from a set to its power set can be surjective.
commonMistakes: Confusing the theorem with Cantor's diagonal argument on real numbers (which is a specific application).
Assuming the theorem only applies to finite sets, when its main power is in dealing with infinite sets.
